title: Chapter 9 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of Chapter 9 concepts!

questions.question: What is a major advantage of a `struct` over an array?
options: Structs can store items dynamically without sizing.
Structs can hold multiple variables of DIFFERENT data types together.
questions.explanation: Arrays can only hold ints, or only strings. Structs assemble ints, strings, and bools into one cohesive bundled object.

questions.question: The variables defined inside a struct block are officially known as struct _____.
questions.explanation: Each distinct variable nested in the structure is a member.

questions.question: To get or set data inside a struct, you use the 'arrow' operator `->` (in standard non-pointer struct access).
questions.explanation: You use the DOT operator `.`, for example `myCar.brand`.

questions.question: In C++, what does a `struct` definition look like at the very end of its curly brace?
questions.explanation: Struct block definitions must terminate cleanly with a semicolon `};`.

questions.question: What does a named structure act like?
options: A blueprint for a custom data type.
questions.explanation: By naming `struct Car...`, you can freely instantiate `Car myCar1; Car myCar2;` throughout your app.

questions.question: An _____ is a special type used to define a list of named constant integers.
questions.explanation: Enumerations (enums) map developer-friendly text constants to numeric values.

questions.question: Enums are a good choice when you have a variable that should strictly only hold one out of a small set of possible values.
questions.explanation: Examples include days of the week, compass directions, or error code flags.

questions.question: If you create `enum Color { RED, GREEN, BLUE };`, what underlying integer is assigned to `GREEN`?
questions.explanation: By default, the list begins counting at 0. So RED=0, GREEN=1, BLUE=2.

questions.question: What happens if you print an enum variable directly with `cout`?
options: It prints the text label of the enum (e.g. "GREEN")
It prints the underlying integer value (e.g. "1")
It throws a string serialization compilation error
questions.explanation: Under the hood, C++ treats enums entirely as their integer counterparts.

questions.question: Can you change the underlying integer value of an enum manually?
options: Yes, using assignment like `RED = 50,` inside the enum block.
questions.explanation: You can explicitly tweak the starting integer, or even every integer precisely to map to custom data.

questions.question: Given `enum Grades { A = 90, B, C };`, what is the value of `C`?
questions.explanation: It auto-increments from previous lines. A=90, B=91, C=92.

questions.question: To group related variables together, use a _____. To group related unchangeable constants together, use an enum.
questions.explanation: Structs handle diverse dynamic properties whereas Enums map a fixed list of labels to integers.

questions.question: Structs inherently hide their members. You can't access `myCar.year` without a password.
questions.explanation: No, standard C++ struct members are completely 'public' and can be modified directly by anyone.

questions.question: Which properly assigns data to a struct variable named `book`?
questions.explanation: The dot operator accesses its internal structure property.

questions.question: Where is the best place to define a `struct` or `enum` so that your whole program can use the blueprint?
options: Inside the most deeply nested for loop.
In the global scope, above main() and other functions.
At the bottom of the file.
questions.explanation: Defining blueprints globally at the top ensures all functions reading top-down can utilize them.

questions.question: Once a variable is created from an enum, it enforces strict type safety by only allowing the assignment of the _____ defined in the original enum list.
questions.explanation: You can't randomly shove '55' into a boolean or an Enum if '55' isn't explicitly defined in its valid scope.

questions.question: You can declare a struct globally, but then you are only allowed to instantiate one single variable from it down in main().
questions.explanation: No! Structs are blueprints. You can manufacture 100 structs from that one master blueprint!

questions.question: What limits how many members a structure can carry?
questions.explanation: Structs can be infinitely massive, housing thousands of variables representing huge datasets.

questions.question: If you compile: `struct { int age; } p1, p2;` what does this do?
options: Creates a struct without a blueprint name, but immediately creates two variables `p1` and `p2` that have that structure.
questions.explanation: You can instantly generate anonymous struct manifestations immediately before the terminating semicolon.

questions.question: Enums are incredibly prevalent in game development. Based on these answers, why?
options: Because games need strings to display text.
Because state management like PLAYER_STATE = JUMPING is dramatically easier to manage with ENUMS than raw integer states like PLAYER_STATE = 42.
Because enums grant faster FPS frame rates.
questions.explanation: Enums convert obscure undocumented number systems into hardcoded bug-free text constants.
